title: Jessica Shulsinger -Services Hiring Manager Interview | Technical Managing Editor
meeting_title: Jessica Shulsinger -Services Hiring Manager Interview | Technical Managing Editor

transcript.text: Hi, Andi.
How are you?
Good.
How are you?
Pretty good.
I'm Monday's starting out.
Pretty good.
I'm on the East Coast, so it's pretty deep in there already.
Yeah.
Where are you located?
Portland, Oregon.
Oh, amazing.
Yeah.
Cool.
Well, I'm very excited.
Thank you for taking the time to chat with me.
We're like hiring for like a couple of people for this role.
I'm really excited to like chat with you and get to know you a little bit.
But I can give you some context on my position and my view of the role.
And then we can talk about you and I'll try and save some time for questions at the end.
Okay, perfect.
Okay, awesome.
So I lead customer operations for GrowthX, which basically means I help like put together the process and all of the pieces that go into delivering content for our clients.
So I am one of the things we're thinking a lot about right now as we're building out an internal and all...

Platform that we're leveraging to generate our content is really making sure that the feedback loop is super strong between what we call the delivery org, which is the client-facing org, and the build org, which is our engineering org.
So this role is, we work within pods for clients.
So this role is essentially kind of wears two hats.
One is like a project management hat, thinking about coordinating all of the pieces for each individual client, and editing kind of like backs up editor of the content that's generated by AI.
And we have content managers kind of as our core layer that runs the workflows.
And then there's a director layer, or account strategist layer above, above the technical managing editor who is
Is helping with kind of like strategic alignment and long-term vision of the engagement.
But I think because this role is like a project manager, there are obviously going to be like client-facing aspects to it.
And I think like depending on a person's skill set, we do have like managing editors who are like, I don't want to talk to people.
Like I love the editorial part of my job and I like the workflows.
And then we have people that, you know, like to like brainstorm with clients and be really involved and talking to them.
So we want to kind of start to strike a balance of people who are willing to do both.
But that's where we are today.
And then, yeah, this role will report up to me.
And I think like there's a lot of opportunity to like influence the way that the product is being built.
Based on what you're doing.
So that's one of the more exciting things I think about the shape of this role is taking.
So do you have any questions based on that?
I don't think so.
No, that was great in terms of like sort of overview and like an org sort of layout.
That's good.
Perfect.
So why don't you tell me a little bit about yourself?
Sure.
Yeah.
Um, so I'm a writer and editor kind of at my core, right?
That's been who the through line of my profession.
Um, every role I've held has been connected to content and publishing and writing in some way.
Um, most recently and relevantly, I worked at a marketing studio.
Um, we were a copywriting agency only, so we didn't have like a design side.
So in that sense, we weren't like a traditional marketing agency.
Um, and I was actually recruited there.
Um, I'd done some freelance work for the agency and they called me up, um, um,
A year later, they were like, our business is doubling.
Do you want to come on board?
And I did, and they brought me on as an on-staff editor, which they hadn't had, and then I became the managing editor.
And I held that role for nine years.
So I was a managing editor for the studio, and we were a tiny studio, 26 people.
So we actually did quite a bit of client-facing work.
So in terms of like that aspect, you the responsibility, in this role, I'm pretty comfortable there.
We, you know, would often join the producers, the PM team, when we were pitching or, you know, aligning around new projects or kickoffs, stuff like that.
But then most prominently, I managed the adidas.com account.
So we created all the content for PDPs.
And then later on, we did all of the product landing page content for adidas.com for two decades.
And I was head of content for that for about nine years.
And then very relevantly to this role, in 2020, adidas asked us to start
Using AI for the content creation, which is kind of pre-AI, kind of like exploding into the content world on a larger scale with ChatGPT a couple of years later.
We actually partnered with a company called Hypotenuse AI, which is a product copy focused AI that's based in Singapore.
And I worked with Joshua Wong pretty closely, who is the founder of Hypotenuse.
He actually knows Jason Gong, who's on staff here at GrowthX.
So I mentioned that to Joshua and he chatted with Jason about me a little bit.

But so being with Joshua when he was really starting that company and having Adidas as like a big client for them kind of from, you know, from the start and then being in on, I I'm not an engineer, but on that, you know, I sort of sat between.
So we worked with Hypotenuse.
Adidas didn't, you know, ask us to work with Hypotenuse.
So I was kind of in the middle of that sort of triangulated.

Relationship, keeping Adidas updated on, you know, kind of how Gen AI at that enterprise scale worked and how we were really building out, you know, what were very large scale prompts and running off of a lot of data from Adidas to create product copy and SEO longer PLP content.
Later on, we created blogs for them too, using some similar tools.

And then watching, I mean, the amazingly fast improvements that the Hypotenuse team, you know, through training and retraining and feedback loops that we had with the LLM that they use, like watching it just improve across all product categories for Adidas was incredible.
So I knew the power of like Gen AI pretty early on, I feel like in terms of like how the rest of the content world adapted it.
So I'm really looking to just harness and leverage that experience that I had, you know, those last like four years and step into a role with what I feel like is a pretty good.
The base.
I've been thinking about this role.
We had that one big client, right?
Adidas.
used AI in smaller ways across the agency.
But I'm thinking this is, you know, GrowthX seems to have 50 Adidas, basically.
Right?

Where you're just creating content from large scale prompts and using, you know, the systems that you're creating, like you're talking about guiding sort of the creation of the tool on the platform is extremely exciting to me, having kind of been in that.
And been in those conversations, seeing what a client like Adidas wants, right?
Like a global corporation.
And you guys work with like Reddit, those kinds of sizes of corporations and the content demand that they have.
And understanding how to fit that using these tools is kind of exactly where I would love to be.
That's why this role just seems, you know, just super exciting.
I'm really drawn to it.
I'm just stepping right out of where I was into something like this would be pretty fantastic.
Yeah, I mean, I would love to dive a little bit more.
Lauren, to that, it seems like such an interesting place to be in the middle between Hypotenuse and Adidas.
Like, what content were you generating using the AI?
And, like, what were the, like, human intervention feedback loops?
And also, like, curious a little bit about the role you guys played in prompting.
Sure, yeah.
So the company that I, the marketing agency I worked at, it's called Thread.
The owner had a sister, a partner, like, tech company called Talkoot, which was a CMS.
And he designed CMS with some friends to basically, you know, be a platform for all of Adidas.com content to be created in.
so it was a, you know, to some degree, it was a collaborative tool.
know, the client could view the content.
We could see their feedback, you know, in real time in the tool.
And then my team could also communicate in the tool, right?
Right.
And hypotenuse, the API was with the CMS.
So the prompts that we used were actually the product data for the products.
So we wrote all the product descriptions for all the products on adidas.com.
And the prompts were so and within the hypotenuse AI writer, there were product categories.
So there was, the adidas has all these brands, so adidas originals was their style category.
So we had trained that particular writer with, I mean, you know, did an initial training with thousands of product descriptions that our team had written.
So we knew we were capturing, you know, amazing content, right, and training the tool in that way.
And then we trained with a lot of brand, you know, voice documents, a lot of historical documents, a lot of specific stuff like that.
So so there were different product categories.
So global football.
was a very specific and challenging product category.

Adidas had a lot of legal regulations about how we could talk about their technology, what kind of claims we could have in copy about how a product will improve an athlete's performance, what kind of historical events we could and couldn't talk about.
So all of that was built into the AI writer, the tool of LLM.
Um, and, um, we would, you know, initially give a lot of feedback directly to hypotenuse.
And then eventually we got to a place where we were working more with like monthly meetings and sort of spreadsheets that we would track particular issues in.
Um, the tool ran, you could run like 50 or a hundred models at a time.
You could just like hit a button.
Um, and, or you could run one at a time and it would give you like two or three options and you could literally be like, this one's good.
This one's not good.
And it would do an instant kind of feedback loop.
And you couldn't, you couldn't, there was no direct prompting that our team could do with the tool.
So the most you could do is like rerun.
That was kind of it.
But we were working with pretty short output for product copy.
was 300 characters to 500 characters.
So it was short.
It wasn't, you know, in terms of like rewriting, we're not talking about a blog link.
The PLP content that we created, the product landing page content was different because it wasn't so, so there was this accuracy piece with product copy that it had to be, has to be accurate, right?
You can't say you're selling, you know, a flip-flop if you're selling a brand, right?
So it has to be a thousand percent accurate.
So most of what our team did was accuracy.
And some, you know, some rewriting content for tone of voice or, you know, creative content or that kind of thing.
PLP content was much broader.
So it was more, you know, traditional SEO, like a consumer search for, you know, brand new running shoes for.
And so we worked with a lot of long tail SEO that had to be built into the landing page content, but it was just broader content.

So it was, I would say that project was easier and perhaps more of a one-time one with some of the content that I feel like the clients that you're working with, maybe, I mean, there's that accuracy piece.
I have watched a lot of content that Marcel has put out around, you know, the fact checkers and all the different tools you have built in.
And we never got to that place with our tool.
But, you know, I would love to see how those kind of tools work.
So I have experience kind of working in that longer form content as well.
Yeah.
As that real specific, very, like, accuracy-heavy sort of output.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I mean, it sounds familiar just in terms of, like, we think about artifacts that go into our workflow.
So, like, we have templated workflows that are, like, okay, these are the four pieces of inputs and the four, like, processes that run before generate the article, right?
So, like...
like...
We have a brief workflow, but the article brief takes into account, like, company context, persona information, like, tone and brand, sort of brand voice artifacts.
And so, like, you know, this role really builds, like, the background pieces that then feed into the automated workflow.
So that certainly translates.

I'm curious, like, did you start at all to think about, or did Adidas start to ask you guys about, like, the other side of SEO, the new side of SEO, which is, like, geo is what we're calling it, but, like, LLM results and, like, for that?
Or did you work with hypotenuse on that at all?
No, not really at all.
And we're not either.
Like, we're, like, this is, it's, like, that's, like, the cutting edge of every question that people ask, so I was just curious.
Mm-hmm.
Yeah.
Cool.
Yeah.
So it sounds like Adidas was the biggest client, but can you talk to me about, did you manage a lot of clients at once?
know you, it sounds like you kind of like managed content for a team.
So talk to me about like your workload balancing and prioritizing in your role.
Sure.
Yeah.
Probably the number of clients that we managed kind of annually hit around 10 or 12.
We had a lot of, you know, pretty long-term clients.
Adidas was about 65% of our revenue and everything else was in that, the rest of that, you know, percentage bucket.
I would say, you know, so I was, it was my role, was managing editor.
And so my role was, I was heavily involved at like kickoff.
And with handover documents, organizing all the style guides for our team, making sure that all the resources were, you know, accessible.

So I was involved, you know, at that sort of point of client facing, just aligning around, you know, tone of voice, what they, we would sort of look at, if they were sort of looking for a brand refresh or a voice refresh, we would look at like, what kind of, you know, content is out there?
what content did they like?
What length?
What?
So sort of that.
And then we would sort of present content back to them, present style guide back to them.
So I was pretty involved at kickoff.
And I would say in terms of like ongoing client management, our PM team or like producer team did most of the sort of, you know, weekly or monthly check-ins or whatever we were doing.
I would come in to client calls when there was a question about a specific deliverable or a question about like, why was the content written?
Thank
And I was sort of, the producer team would turn to me and be like, well, why'd we do that?
Like, right.
And so I would come in and I would explain like, well, this particular content needs to stay in this tone of voice or using these references for a PDP.
And it doesn't belong because what you're asking for actually is marketing language, or it's actually something for social or something for a campaign.

Or, you know, so those kind of sort of brought in, not as sort of like a mediator, exactly, but kind of like to really fully explain why our writers had chosen this storytelling and not that storytelling.
So I did that sort of client facing a lot.
You know, in terms of balancing and prioritizing, we use project management tools.
We used Hive.
Yeah.
And that really structured our, we sort of booked our work two weeks out.
I mean, looking ahead and sort of seeing what I had across a week or a month, we had Slack, we had team channels.
I was also in charge of.
assigning work out to the team.
So I really was aware of how our team worked and then where my work fit into it.
And then when we had issues come up, how different types of content, like what could be pushed out?
What would actually, if we allowed this much time for this project, I knew it's not going to take that long.
It could take this long and this person can do it.
So I was really good at shifting stuff.
That was a large focus of mine the last couple of years I was with the company.

And then in terms of my own work, know, and more recently in the last like year or two, I did a lot of, we were just working a lot with the U is doing a lot of like thought leadership, a lot of presentation around, you know, sort of the project.
So I did a lot of communication that was just stakeholder facing.
Yeah.
And so a lot of presentations, a lot of decks that I was just building.
And those are going to be kind of long term, right?
So research, collaborating with the team, creating the content, revising, bring back from the client.
So that sort of work was where I was more.
Focus in terms of my own production and then management of the teams.
Yeah, that makes sense.
I probably should have asked this earlier, so I'm going to look back to the AI thing.
just think the hypotenuse example is clearly such a strong correlation to the work that we're doing.
I'm curious, what was your interaction with the technical teams and how did you structure?
That feedback loop and how much into the product did you get?
Yeah, sure.
That's a great question.
Our team was, in terms of in the moment kind of technical staff, our team was primarily in the U.S.
and then a little bit in Europe.
They were in Singapore.
So there was this real-time delay that I imagine you're probably not facing too much.
So that was tough to manage sometimes.
Sometimes in terms of like our team's expectations, like if a tool would just stop working.
Or we did the Takut team, the CMS team could sort of in the moment pinch hit and sort of solve some problems.

So like if all of the fields that the AI needed to run off of weren't filled out, you know, that was something that we could sort of pitch hit with our CMS team and sort of like figure out why something wasn't running.
We sort of, you know, there were different components to the content we were delivering.
So our team, we could guide them to why don't work in this area instead of this.
And when Hypotenuse is online, we can sort of figure out what's going on there.
Yeah, so I would say like that is, that was kind of, was our like our technical role.
And then in terms of, you know, was really involved in like the training of it initially.
So I think probably where it kind of relates is when you're talking about like those template docs, maybe, or like the briefs that you're using to like train the LLMs.
I mean, we sort of talked about with Hypotenuse.
Oh, you know, we talked a lot actually about hierarchy of sort of inputs, right?
So for the different categories that we worked in, we could switch with, you know, creating a hierarchy for different.
So there was just certain sort of probably very superficial stuff that I kind of understood how the output.
But the other thing, just to, like, mention really briefly, I worked on with Hypotonics, we created an AI, a product copy AI writer tool for our agency specifically.
And in that tool, we worked more with direct prompting.
So we worked with, you know, create a product description that is specifically for a trail writing show, da, da, da, da.
And there we could really see, you know, the impact of specific prompts on copy and how that worked in addition, you know, in conjunction with running off of product data.
And system information, so kind of superficial, but I sort of knew when something was going wrong, who to ask with the sort of timeframe.
And was about how to fix it, and kind of what to do, maybe going forward about how to better solve it.

Yeah, mean, I think that's the important piece, right, is like, it's not like anybody on the delivery side is writing the code, or like, jumping into the code, but it's like, how do you give feedback that the engineering team can action on?
Yeah.
And like, say, like, okay, this is where it's, like, this is where the process is breaking down.
Okay, seven steps, like, I get it to here, and then like, I need to do this, which is a little bit different than what it is.

Yeah, like, they would need to know what literally is like the URL that where it broke down or something like that, like they would want the specificity, so I need to capture that with whoever was reporting the problem to me.
Yeah, right.
Cool.
Well, we have seven minutes left.
So what question is this for me?
Sure.
You're, I mean, GrowthX has done a lot of hiring.
What kind of, you talked a little bit about sort of the org and sort of like where this role kind of fits.

How has it been to kind of bring on, have you brought on, not you literally, but like the companies brought on, you know, I would imagine folks since you've come in, and how are you managing that, like at a sort of a startup, in sort of a startup organization?

Yeah, so I was, I think, one of the last, in one of the last big batches of hires, there was a period in the early spring, I would say, when like we hired, our eyes were bigger than our stomach, sort of, we like, we had, we got all these like really exciting new clients, Marcel, who's our CEO, was like amazing at like selling a vision, and closing deals, and he is the person that like, if you've watched his content, like, you know, like, he has figured out how a lot of this stuff works before, like, I'm ready to hand it off.
But when you sign like 20 clients,
A month.
And then you hire like 30 people in the same month.
Then you kind of have to like, say, okay, this is here's the content that I have for what we've developed, like figure it out.
Yeah.
Where we struggled this spring was in the training for onboarding the teams.
And we're being much more thoughtful in like, onboarding and also just like, you know, vetting our, our potential employees.

Um, so I think like the hardest part has been because everybody got thrown into the process and they wanted to like do a good job and like, and we didn't have our internal platform totally built out yet.
We were using air ops workflows.
Like a lot of people were like, get it done any way I can.
And so they either fell back.

On manual, literally writing articles, we have people with a really strong experience in those areas, or they would just create projects in Claude or ChatGPT, and again, not have that learning loop for our internal engineering team and tooling.
And so there was a period where it was like, get it done any way you can, nobody really knows the best way to do that.
And so I think the hardest part of the last few months for me has been trying to put everything back in a box and be like, now we have a platform, which is a super cool, amazing platform.
Our engineers are so, so talented, and it's amazing, but it is still six weeks old, so like, it's not perfect.
It's like, there is a...
Learning curve.
And we have, like, client deliverables.

So, like, asking people to come back to the process, use the tools that, like, we've built for them, and give us feedback about what is and isn't working when they're used to, like, just hacking it together in whatever way they can.
Is, it's an adjustment, right?
I, like, I think we're doing, we've made so, so much progress.
But that has been, like, just that mind shift has been a challenge for the whole team.
Mm-hmm.
Mm-hmm.
Yeah.
Oh, thanks for that.
Yeah, thanks for that being that, you know, transparent about that.

That's really, it's kind of cool to hear, like, you know, it's kind of exciting in, like, a certain way, but I fully understand, like, yeah, that's a lot of moving pieces, a lot of complexity, a lot of unanticipated.
Sort of, like, situations.
Yeah.
I mean, I have faith in where we're going, so I'm happy to, like, tell you about what hurt to get.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
A thousand percent.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.

What sort of like, if you're sort of looking to the future now that you've got like the platform kind of more like on board and, you know, online, what is kind of like for this role, or I know that there's several managing editors like on staff, what does sort of a day or week look like in terms of like structured time?
Like I know that there's like a four hour overlap with Pacific Coast.
I would imagine that's when there's any kind of like all hands or team meetings.
And then beyond that, there's probably, I'm kind of answering my own questions, probably like, you know, role meetings and pod meetings.
And then, but sort of like those, I guess I'm more interested in maybe like the meetings with like the engineering team and sort of those feedback and then work sessions and then with client.
What have been kind of the client, you know, schedule and sort of like that page.
In terms of like weekly and monthly, and how have those relationships kind of gone to date?
Yeah, so our managing editors are managing like three to five accounts per person.
And like they have, you know, between one and three content managers who are running the work for them.

So for each client, we're depending on, you know, if we have a workflow that's like, we have a client right now where we can press a button and like refresh 100 articles on their website in like a day.
And so but that's like, we have the engineering workflow that took a couple of weeks for us to build and refine.
We also, you know, have clients where we haven't automated publishing in their CMS yet.
So we're publishing like five to seven articles a week, which, you know, still pretty good pace.
So there, it depends on like how integrated and what their contract looks like.
In terms of, like, content production.
And we use Airtable to manage that, like, in terms of a back and forth.
And then we have a Slack channel with every client.
In terms of, like, the makeup of a day, we also talk to most clients once a week.
So you're probably having three to five client meetings a week.
You know, the director should be leading most of those.
But, you know, there is, like, a status update and a little bit of reporting that I think the managing editor is taking the lead on.
And then as, like, strategy shifts, helping figure out, like, okay, now how do we deliver this?
Because I think, you know, we, most of our deals are relatively young.
Like, most of our accounts, like, are relatively young in terms of the relationship.
So we are doing a lot of, like, delivering.

Against original scope of work, but we're starting to get to the phase where it's like, okay, we've like put out a couple hundred, you know, we're getting to the point where, okay, it's time to like reexamine where this goes.
And so I think like, that's kind of the next thing that we're thinking about is how do we shift and help keep clients like on the cutting edge.
That was why I asked about like geo.
That's why clients are asking a lot now.
And it's like, okay, we have to think about like, revamping the FAQs and like making sure that the page is chunkable and things like that.
So I would like that was a bit of a rambling answer.
But those are like the pieces that go into like a weak person's role.
Yeah, no, there's a lot of content in there that was like, totally applicable.
And yeah, really useful.
That's fantastic.
Yeah, the GEO.
mean, I'm familiar with it from I did listen to there was a interview with Jason and somebody that was recently around GEO.
And so that's, yeah, we really didn't, haven't worked in that, but I have a sort of basic understanding of it from, from that and kind of from what you've said to you.
Yeah, that interview was so, so useful.
Yeah, yeah.
We keep pointing people to it, so.
Yeah, yeah.
I think like our goal is to be on the cutting edge of that, but.
Right, for sure.
But nobody really knows the answer.
I know, well, yeah, you're sort of like here, right?
You're at the edge of.
Yeah, like develop a point of view and we'll be right.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah, I mean, we're at time and a little bit over, but I just want to say thanks a lot for your time.

just, again, I'm really excited about, you know, what I saw kind of like at the end of my time with my agency was kind of just exactly this, just this need for content and really moving toward that, you know, toward GEO and sort of toward what is the content doing now, right?
What is, what is going on with it?
And sort of seeing that kind of as my role that I was kind of wrapping up.
So, so it's just been super exciting to have this conversation and to hear about, you know.
I'll learn.
about your company and where you're going.
So thank you so much for your time and looking forward to hearing more.
Yeah.
Thanks, Jessica.
Have a good day.
The record.

## Meeting Purpose

[Discuss the Technical Managing Editor role at GrowthX with candidate Jessica Shulsinger](https://fathom.video/share/xcketEgx_2FJG2CgfxXEEaj8uBxb9d96?tab=summary&timestamp=105.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Jessica has extensive experience in AI-driven content creation, particularly with Adidas and Hypotenuse AI](https://fathom.video/share/xcketEgx_2FJG2CgfxXEEaj8uBxb9d96?tab=summary&timestamp=416.0)
  - [GrowthX is rapidly growing, managing multiple high-profile clients with AI-powered content generation](https://fathom.video/share/xcketEgx_2FJG2CgfxXEEaj8uBxb9d96?tab=summary&timestamp=159.0)
  - [The role involves project management, editing, client interaction, and providing feedback to the engineering team](https://fathom.video/share/xcketEgx_2FJG2CgfxXEEaj8uBxb9d96?tab=summary&timestamp=197.0)
  - [GrowthX is navigating challenges of rapid growth and implementing a new internal content platform](https://fathom.video/share/xcketEgx_2FJG2CgfxXEEaj8uBxb9d96?tab=summary&timestamp=1514.0)

## Topics

### Role Overview and Company Structure

  - [Technical Managing Editor role combines project management and content editing](https://fathom.video/share/xcketEgx_2FJG2CgfxXEEaj8uBxb9d96?tab=summary&timestamp=197.0)
  - [Reports to Andi Bailey, who leads customer operations](https://fathom.video/share/xcketEgx_2FJG2CgfxXEEaj8uBxb9d96?tab=summary&timestamp=286.0)
  - [Works within client-focused pods, managing 3-5 accounts](https://fathom.video/share/xcketEgx_2FJG2CgfxXEEaj8uBxb9d96?tab=summary&timestamp=197.0)
  - [Coordinates with content managers, directors, and account strategists](https://fathom.video/share/xcketEgx_2FJG2CgfxXEEaj8uBxb9d96?tab=summary&timestamp=229.0)
  - [Opportunity to influence product development based on delivery experiences](https://fathom.video/share/xcketEgx_2FJG2CgfxXEEaj8uBxb9d96?tab=summary&timestamp=292.0)

### Jessica's Relevant Experience
